text: for a moment, then said in a voice as lifeless as her eyes, “You’re new.” Frank didn’t know what to say to that so he just stared back. Finally the girl pointed to a sign tacked to the front of the booth that read “1 Dollar per Carload.” Frank frowned; surely that couldn’t be right. But when he handed over a five, the girl gave him four ones as change. He thanked her, and as he pulled away from the booth he thought he heard her say, “You shouldn’t have stopped.” “Well, she was a friendly sort, wasn’t she?” he grumbled. Sheryl shrugged. “Probably sore she has to work on a Saturday night while her friends no doubt are enjoying the double-feature.” Frank coasted along, the branches of the trees that lined either side of the dirt road reaching across to create a virtual ceiling above the car. It wasn’t yet full dark, but the foliage gave the impression that Frank was driving through a tunnel. At least until he rounded a slight curve and ended up in a clearing that contained the dirt lot as well as the massive white screen and a concession stand at the far end. The area was large, with enough spaces for at least fifty cars, most of which were already occupied. “Whoa,” Frank said, stopping at the entrance to the very back row. Sheryl glanced out the windshield. “Popular place.” “Yeah, not to mention…peculiar.” “What do you mean?” “Sheryl, take a good look around. At the cars, the people. Don’t you notice anything a little strange?” Sheryl leaned forward and studied the drive-in. Frank saw the realization dawn in her eyes and she grunted a puzzled, “Hmpf.” It wasn’t the sight of so many cars that gave one pause, but the type of cars. From what Frank could see, his 2019 Camry was the only vehicle in the lot that wasn’t at least fifty or sixty years old. All the cars were older models, the large boxy types that looked as if they could house families of four or more. But despite their age, the vehicles looked to be in near-mint condition. And yet even more peculiar than the cars were the people. Groups milled about outside their vehicles, flitting from car to car to visit and chat. Most of them were teenagers, which wasn’t so unusual, but what was unusual was how they were dressed. The girls were wearing mostly jumpers or poodle skirts and sweaters. The boys fell into one of two camps: tight jeans, leather jackets, greasy hair done up in exaggerated pompadours, or preppied out in slacks and button-up shirts, short hair combed with precision. Frank felt like he’d stepped into that old movie Pleasantville , which in turn was about stepping into an old TV show. “Let’s do the time warp again,” he said softly. Beside him, Sheryl clapped her hands together and giggled delightedly. “Oh, they must be having a theme night. How fun.” “Theme night?” “Sure. Since they’re showing the James Dean movies, they must be having 50s night, probably advertised it in the paper or something. That would explain the cheap admission price and the outfits.” Frank scanned the lot again. “But what about the cars? Do you really think all these kids just happened to have access to vintage cars?” Sheryl dismissed the question with a shrug. “It looks like we picked a good night to find this place.” Frank couldn’t help but smile at his wife’s enthusiasm and excitement. Her promise had him pretty excited himself. He drove down the back row to the very last space and pulled in. The spots around him were unoccupied and he hoped they stayed that way. He stared out at the metal pole next to the car. “That’s odd.” “What?” Sheryl said, running her fingers up and down his forearm, something she knew drove him wild. “Um, just that most drive-ins these days pipe the sound in through the car radio, but this place still has the old-timey speakers you perch on your window.” “Well, I think we should keep the windows rolled up for a while. They might even get all fogged over.” Again Frank looked at his wife as if she were a stranger, but one he very much wanted to get to know. “If I’d had any idea going to a drive-in would have this effect on you, I’d have taken you years ago.” “Maybe we’ll have to become repeat customers.” “I could be up for that.” Sheryl placed a hand on his crotch and squeezed. “I’ll say.” On the far side of the lot, the towering screen exploded to life in a blaze of color and sound, and Frank leaned his head against the back of the seat as his wife unzipped his pants. # Twenty minutes later, Frank wiped sweat from his forehead with the back of a hand, panting for breath as if he’d just run five miles. “Sweet Jesus,” he gasped. “I mean, that was just…sweet Jesus.” Next to him, Sheryl was digging through her purse. She finally found a tissue and wiped away her smudged lipstick then smiled at her husband. “Glad you enjoyed it. Now I could use some water.” “What?” “Water. I suddenly have the overwhelming desire to rinse out my mouth.” “Want me to go grab a bottle from the concession stand?” “I’d say it’s the least you can do after all I did for you.” “Hey, no argument from me,” Frank said, tucking himself in and zipping up. “Want anything else while I’m going?” “Well, since we’re going to be staying for both films, you might as well get us some sodas and popcorn. Biggest, butteriest tub they’ve got.” “I’m on it.” Frank opened the door and stepped out then turned and leaned back into the car. “You’re amazing, you know that?” “Yeah, I know,” Sheryl said, flicking her tongue out playfully. “I just have to remind you of it every once in a
